common.noFilterResults: No results for the selected filters.

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.description: Overview of your vulnerability landscape across all visible namespaces.
dashboard.helpBody1: The Dashboard provides a real-time overview of your vulnerability posture across all visible namespaces. It aggregates CVE data into charts and metrics so you can quickly assess risk without browsing individual CVEs.
dashboard.helpBody2ChartsDesc: — Severity distribution, EPSS risk matrix, cluster heatmap, trend lines, fixability breakdown, and more. Click chart segments to navigate to filtered CVE lists.
dashboard.helpBody2StatsDesc: — Total CVEs, active escalations, fixable critical CVEs, open risk acceptances, and overdue deadlines at a glance.
dashboard.helpBody2PriorityDesc: — Dedicated tables for CVEs marked as priority by the security team and those with the highest exploitation probability.
dashboard.helpBody3: Security team members see organization-wide data. Regular users see only CVEs in their permitted namespaces, filtered by CVSS and EPSS thresholds.
dashboard.priorityCvesDescription: These CVEs have been prioritized by the security team and should be addressed first.
dashboard.highEpssDescription: These CVEs have the highest probability of being exploited in the next 30 days.
dashboard.noHighEpss: No CVEs with high EPSS score
dashboard.epssMatrixDescription: Each dot is a CVE. Top right = immediate action required.
dashboard.mttr: Mean Time to Remediate by Severity

help.epssMatrix: The EPSS Risk Matrix plots each CVE by CVSS severity (Y-axis) and EPSS exploitation probability (X-axis). CVEs in the top-right have both high severity and high exploitation likelihood and should be addressed first.
help.pipeline: Shows the current distribution of all risk acceptances by status. Quickly see how many requests are pending, approved, rejected, or expired.
help.severityDistribution: Shows the distribution of your visible CVEs by severity level. Click a segment to filter the vulnerability list accordingly.
help.fixabilityBreakdown: Shows how many of your CVEs can be fixed by an update vs. those with no fix available yet. Helps prioritize update efforts.
help.fixableTrend: Shows the trend of fixable vs. unfixable CVEs over the last 30 days. Rising trends in unfixable CVEs require special attention.
help.clusterHeatmap: Shows CVE distribution across clusters by severity. Clusters with many critical CVEs should be reviewed first.
help.mttr: Shows the average time in days from when a CVE was first seen to resolution, grouped by severity. This captures the full exposure window. Only completed remediations are included.
help.aging: Shows how long CVEs have been known. Old, unaddressed CVEs represent increased risk and should be prioritized.
help.trend: Shows the number of newly discovered CVEs per day over the last 90 days, broken down by severity. Only CVEs first seen within this period are included. CVEs known for longer still appear in the CVE list but not in this chart.
help.cvesPerNamespace: Shows the top 10 namespaces with the most CVEs, broken down by severity. Helps identify which namespaces need the most attention.
help.topVulnerableComponents: Shows the 10 components with the most CVEs, split into fixable and unfixable. Updating these components can resolve many CVEs at once.

cves.title: Vulnerabilities
cves.description: All CVEs affecting your deployments, filterable by severity, EPSS, and fixability.
cves.helpBody1: This page lists every CVE affecting your visible deployments. Use the filters to narrow down by severity, CVSS/EPSS ranges, fixability, component, namespace, risk acceptance status, or remediation status.
cves.helpBody2ByCveDesc: — Each row is a unique CVE with severity, EPSS score, affected images/deployments, and fix availability. Prioritized CVEs always sort to the top.
cves.helpBody2ByImageDesc: — Groups CVEs by container image so you can see which image updates will resolve the most vulnerabilities at once.
cves.helpBody2ActionsDesc: — From any CVE you can request a risk acceptance, start a remediation, or flag it as a false positive.
cves.helpBody3: Non-security users only see CVEs above the configured CVSS and EPSS thresholds. Prioritized CVEs and CVEs with active risk acceptances are always visible regardless of thresholds.
cves.epssTooltip: Probability of exploitation in the next 30 days (EPSS score from FIRST.org)
cves.noResultsHint: Try adjusting filters or broadening the scope.
cves.priorityBanner: This CVE has been prioritized as {{priority}}.
cves.thresholdHint: Only CVEs with CVSS ≥ {{cvss}} and EPSS ≥ {{epss}}% are shown. Prioritized CVEs and active risk acceptances are always visible.
cves.imageGroupNoCves: No CVEs found for this image

cveDetail.startRemediation: Start remediation from the CVE detail page.
cveDetail.deploymentFilterPlaceholder: Filter by deployment, namespace, cluster or image...
cveDetail.wontFixPlaceholder: Why won't this CVE be fixed?
cveDetail.fpReasonPlaceholder: Why is this CVE a false positive? (min. 10 characters)
cveDetail.hintAssess: Next step: Assess this vulnerability and create a remediation or risk acceptance.
cveDetail.hintPrioritize: Next step: Prioritize this vulnerability to keep it on your radar.
cveDetail.hintRemediate: Next step: Create a remediation or request a risk acceptance.
cveDetail.hintResolve: Next step: All remediations must be completed or risk accepted.
cveDetail.hintVerify: Next step: Verify the completed remediations.

riskAcceptance.title: Risk Acceptances
riskAcceptance.description: Review and manage risk acceptance requests for known vulnerabilities.
riskAcceptance.helpBody1: Risk acceptances let teams formally acknowledge a CVE without fixing it immediately. A team member creates a request from the CVE detail page with a justification and scope, and the security team reviews it.
riskAcceptance.helpBody2RequestedDesc: — Awaiting review by the security team
riskAcceptance.helpBody2ApprovedDesc: — Accepted; the CVE bypasses threshold filtering while active
riskAcceptance.helpBody2RejectedDesc: — Declined; the CVE remains subject to normal visibility rules
riskAcceptance.helpBody2ExpiredDesc: — Past its expiry date and no longer active
riskAcceptance.helpBody3: Scope can be set to all occurrences, specific namespaces, images, or deployments. Risk acceptances can also be bulk-imported via Excel. Only the security team can approve or reject requests.
riskAcceptance.justificationPlaceholder: Describe why this CVE can be accepted...
riskAcceptance.editReviewHint: After editing, the security team must review the risk acceptance again.
riskAcceptance.scopeTargetRequired: Please select at least one scope target.
riskAcceptance.withdrawConfirm: Are you sure? The request will be permanently deleted.
riskAcceptance.noAcceptancesHint: Risk acceptances are created from the CVE detail page.

priorities.title: CVE Priorities
priorities.description: Manually prioritized CVEs remain visible regardless of threshold settings.
priorities.helpBody1: The security team can manually prioritize CVEs that require urgent attention. Prioritized CVEs are always visible to all users regardless of CVSS/EPSS threshold settings and always sort to the top of the vulnerability list.
priorities.helpBody2CriticalDesc: — Highest urgency, typically actively exploited or business-critical
priorities.helpBody2HighDesc: — Significant risk, should be addressed promptly
priorities.helpBody2MediumDesc: — Notable risk, plan remediation within a reasonable timeframe
priorities.helpBody2LowDesc: — Lower urgency, but still flagged for visibility
priorities.helpBody3: Each priority includes a reason and an optional deadline. Only the security team can set, update, or remove priorities. Prioritized CVEs appear with a banner on their detail page.
priorities.reasonPlaceholder: Why is this CVE being prioritized?
priorities.noPrioritiesHint: The security team can prioritize CVEs from the vulnerability list.
priorities.badgeTooltip: Prioritized — always visible regardless of thresholds.

escalations.title: Escalations
escalations.description: CVEs automatically escalated due to unresolved exposure over time.
escalations.helpBody1: Escalations are triggered automatically when CVEs remain unaddressed for a certain period. Escalation levels depend on severity, EPSS score, and the age of the vulnerability.
escalations.helpBody3: Upcoming escalations show CVEs that will reach a level in the coming days. Address them in time to avoid escalation. CVEs with active risk acceptances are not escalated.
escalations.upcomingCount: {{count}} CVE(s) are about to be escalated

remediations.title: Remediations
remediations.description: Track CVE fix progress per namespace from creation to verification.
remediations.helpBody1: Remediations track active CVE resolution. A team member creates a remediation for a CVE in their namespace and tracks progress until verification by the security team.
remediations.helpBody2ResolvedDesc: — CVE has been fixed, awaiting verification
remediations.helpBody3: Remediations can be automatically marked as resolved when the CVE is no longer found in the namespace's deployments.
remediations.noFilterResults: No remediations for the selected filters.
remediations.createHint: Remediations can be created from the CVE detail page.

badges.title: SVG Badges
badges.description: Embeddable SVG badges showing CVE counts for READMEs and dashboards.
badges.helpBody1: Badges are embeddable SVG images that display live CVE counts. They can be included in repository READMEs, wikis, or dashboards to provide at-a-glance vulnerability status without requiring authentication.
badges.helpBody2ScopeDesc: — Create a general badge for all CVEs, or scope it to a specific namespace and cluster for targeted reporting.
badges.helpBody2TokenDesc: — Each badge has a unique token used in its URL. The badge endpoint is unauthenticated so it can be embedded anywhere.
badges.helpBody2EmbedDesc: — Copy the Markdown snippet or the raw URL to embed the badge. The badge updates automatically as CVE counts change.
badges.helpBody3: When BADGE_BASE_URL is configured, badge URLs are fully qualified for external use. Any user can create badges; they are scoped by creator plus optional namespace and cluster.
badges.label: Label
badges.hint: Badges can be embedded in READMEs without authentication.
badges.noBadgesHint: Create a badge to embed CVE counts in your project docs.
badges.createHint: Leave empty for a general badge. Namespace+Cluster for a namespace-specific badge.

settings.title: Settings
settings.thresholdHint: CVEs below both thresholds are hidden from users (not from the security team). Prioritized CVEs are always visible.
settings.thresholdHelpBody: CVEs that fall below both thresholds (CVSS and EPSS) are hidden from non-security users. Manually prioritized CVEs and CVEs with active risk acceptances remain always visible.
settings.escalationRulesHelpBody: Each rule defines at which severity and EPSS value a CVE will be escalated. If a CVE remains unaddressed (no remediation or risk acceptance), it progresses through levels L1 → L2 → L3 after the configured days. Namespace owners are notified by email; at L3, the management email is also notified.
settings.warningDaysHelpBody: Affected users receive an email notification when a CVE escalation is about to reach the next level within the configured days. This allows time to fix the CVE or submit a risk acceptance before escalation.
settings.digestDayHelpBody: On the selected weekday, a summary of all open CVEs, pending escalations, and risk acceptances is automatically sent by email to the affected namespace owners.
settings.managementEmailHelpBody: Recipient of the weekly management overview and escalation notifications at the highest level (L3). Typically the central security team address or a distribution list to be informed about critical, unresolved CVEs.
settings.sendDigestHelpBody: Sends the digest email immediately to all namespace owners, regardless of the configured weekday.

onboarding.title: Welcome to RHACS CVE Manager
onboarding.description: Your central tool for managing and prioritizing container vulnerabilities across your OpenShift environment.

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.desc: EPSS risk matrix, cluster heatmap, and vulnerability trends at a glance

cves.title: Vulnerabilities
cves.desc: Browse, filter, and prioritize CVEs by EPSS probability

risk.title: Risk Acceptances
risk.desc: Request and manage acceptances with scope-based control

escalations.title: Escalations
escalations.desc: Automatic notifications for overdue critical vulnerabilities

remediations.title: Remediations
remediations.desc: Track remediation status per namespace and document progress

priorities.title: Priorities
priorities.desc: Manually prioritize CVEs to keep them visible regardless of thresholds

suppressions.title: False Positives
suppressions.desc: Create suppression rules for false positives and have them reviewed by the security team

badges.title: Badges
badges.desc: Generate SVG status badges for repository READMEs and dashboards

suppressionRules.title: Suppression Rules (False Positives)
suppressionRules.description: Mark misidentified components or individual CVEs as false positives. Team members request, security team approves.
suppressionRules.helpBody1: Suppression rules mark components or individual CVEs as false positives when scanners misidentify them. A team member requests suppression from the CVE detail page with a reason, and the security team reviews it.
suppressionRules.helpBody2ComponentDesc: — Suppresses all CVEs for a component name, optionally filtered by a version glob pattern (e.g. v0.0.0-* for pseudo-versions).
suppressionRules.helpBody2CveDesc: — Suppresses a specific CVE ID across all or selected namespaces.
suppressionRules.helpBody2ScopeDesc2: — Rules can apply globally or to specific namespaces. Namespace scoping is set from the CVE detail page where affected deployments are visible.
suppressionRules.helpBody3: Approved rules hide matching CVEs from the vulnerability list. Only the security team can approve or reject suppression requests. Rules can be deleted by the creator or by security team members.
suppressionRules.reasonPlaceholder: Why is this a false positive? (e.g., internal module version, not the actual software version)
suppressionRules.versionPatternHint: Glob pattern, e.g. v0.0.0-* for all pseudo-versions. Leave empty for all versions.
suppressionRules.noRulesHint: Request false positives from the CVE detail page.
suppressionRules.scopeNamespaceHint: Namespace selection is available on the CVE detail page, where affected deployments are visible.

scope.title: Scope
scope.tooltip: Filter all views to a specific cluster and namespace.

tour.sidebarNav: Use the sidebar to navigate to all sections: Dashboard, Vulnerabilities, Remediations, Risk Acceptances, and more.
tour.scopeSelector: Use the scope filter to narrow all views to a specific cluster and namespace.
tour.navDashboard: The Dashboard gives you an overview of key metrics and vulnerabilities.
tour.navCves: Here you'll find all vulnerabilities affecting your namespaces. You can prioritize, create risk acceptances, and start remediations.
tour.navRemediations: Track the remediation status of vulnerabilities per namespace and cluster.
tour.navRiskAcceptances: Create and manage risk acceptances for vulnerabilities that are intentionally not fixed.
tour.navPriorities: Set manual priorities for specific CVEs so they always appear at the top of the list.
tour.navEscalations: Escalate vulnerabilities to responsible teams or contacts via email.
tour.navBadges: Create status badges you can embed in dashboards or documentation.
tour.navSuppressionRules: Define rules to hide specific CVEs from views.
tour.navSettings: Configure global settings like EPSS thresholds and email templates (security team only).
tour.navAuditLog: View all security-relevant actions in the audit log (security team only).
tour.helpButton: Click this icon anytime to replay this tour.
